Ayahs

الٓمٓ (١)
Alif, Lam, Meem. (1)
تَنزِيلُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ مِن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٢)
The [gradual] sending down of the Book, there is no doubt in it, is from the Lord of all the worlds. (2)
أَمْ يَقُولُونَ ٱفْتَرَىٰهُ ۚ بَلْ هُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ لِتُنذِرَ قَوْمًا مَّآ أَتَىٰهُم مِّن نَّذِيرٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ (٣)
But they say, ‘He has fabricated it.’ No, it is the truth from your Lord, that you may warn a people to whom there did not come any warner before you, so that they may be guided [to the right path]. (3)
ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا فِى سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ ۖ مَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِهِۦ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا شَفِيعٍ ۚ أَفَلَا تَتَذَكَّرُونَ (٤)
It is Allah who created the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them in six days, then He settled on the Throne. You do not have besides Him any guardian or intercessor. Will you not then take admonition? (4)
يُدَبِّرُ ٱلْأَمْرَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ ثُمَّ يَعْرُجُ إِلَيْهِ فِى يَوْمٍ كَانَ مِقْدَارُهُۥٓ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ مِّمَّا تَعُدُّونَ (٥)
He directs the command from the heaven to the earth; then it ascends toward Him in a day whose span is a thousand years by your reckoning. (5)

The Names of This Surah

It is well-known that this Surah was revealed in Makkah. Some commentators have not excepted any of its verses, while others consider verses 18 to 20 to be "Madani," believing these three verses were revealed in Madinah. However, no evidence or indication of them being Madani is visible within these verses.

In some narrations and in the common language of commentators, the name of this Surah is "Surah al-Sajdah" or "Alif Lam Mim al-Sajdah." Sometimes, to distinguish it from Surah Ha Mim al-Sajdah (Fussilat), it is called "Sajdah Luqman" because it is placed after Surah Luqman.

In some narrations, it is also referred to as "Alif Lam Mim Tanzil."

Fakhr al-Razi and Alusi have also mentioned the name "Surah al-Madaji‘" (The Beds) among its names (in accordance with verse 16 of this Surah: تَتَجافى‌ جُنُوبُهُمْ عَنِ الْمَضاجِعِ‌ ...).

The Virtue of Reciting Surah al-Sajdah

In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated:

"Whoever recites Alif Lam Mim Tanzil and Tabarak al-Ladhi bi-yadihi al-Mulk, it is as if he has spent the Night of Power (Laylat al-Qadr) in worship!" [1]

In another Hadith from Imam Ja'far ibn Muhammad al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is narrated:

"Whoever recites Surah al-Sajdah every Friday night, Allah will give him his record of deeds in his right hand, He will not call him to account for what he has done, and he will be among the companions of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله and his Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام." [1]

Since this Surah contains extensive discussions on the Origin (Mabda') and the Resurrection (Ma'ad), the punishments of the criminals on the Day of Resurrection, and warning lessons related to the believers and the disbelievers, there is no doubt that its recitation—provided it is a recitation that is the source of reflection, and reflection that is the origin of decision-making and action—can so transform a person that they become worthy of all this virtue and honor. Its awakening effect would be like the vigil of the Night of Power, and its result would be placement in the ranks of the "Companions of the Right" (Ashab al-Yamin) and attaining the honor of friendship with the Prophet and his Progeny.

The Content of Surah al-Sajdah

By virtue of being a "Makkan" Surah, this Surah strongly follows the main themes of Makkan Surahs, namely the discussion of the "Origin," the "Resurrection," "Glad Tidings," and "Warnings." Overall, several sections draw attention:

  1. Before anything else, it speaks of the greatness of the Quran, its revelation from the Lord of the Worlds, and the refutation of the enemies' accusations against it.
  2. Then, it discusses the signs of Allah in the heavens and the earth and the governance of this world.
  3. Another discussion concerns the creation of man from "dust," "the water of a drop (semen)," and the "Divine Spirit," and the granting of the means for acquiring knowledge—namely eyes, ears, and intellect—by Allah to him.
  4. After that, it speaks of the Resurrection and the events before it, namely death, and after it, namely questioning and accounting.
  5. and 6. It contains impactful and shaking discussions of glad tidings and warnings; it promises the believers the "Garden of Abode" (Jannat al-Ma'wa) and threatens the transgressors with the punishment of the Fire.
  6. In this context, it makes a brief reference to the history of the Children of Israel, the story of Musa عليه السلام, and the victories of this nation.
  7. Again, in line with the discussion of glad tidings and warnings, it refers to the states of other past nations and their painful fates.
  8. and 10. It returns once more to the issue of "Monotheism (Tawhid) and the signs of Allah's greatness" and concludes the Surah with a "threat to the stubborn enemies."

Thus, the primary goal of the Surah is to strengthen the foundations of faith in the Origin and the Resurrection, to create a powerful wave of movement toward piety, to deter from rebellion and tyranny, and to draw attention to the value of man's high status—which was exceptionally necessary, especially at the beginning of the Islamic movement and in the environment of Makkah.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌ الم (1) تَنْزِيلُ الْكِتابِ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ (2) أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَراهُ بَلْ هُوَ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ لِتُنْذِرَ قَوْماً ما أَتاهُمْ مِنْ نَذِيرٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ (3) اللَّهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ اسْتَوى‌ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَ لا شَفِيعٍ أَ فَلا تَتَذَكَّرُونَ (4) يُدَبِّرُ الْأَمْرَ مِنَ السَّماءِ إِلَى الْأَرْضِ ثُمَّ يَعْرُجُ إِلَيْهِ فِي يَوْمٍ كانَ مِقْدارُهُ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ مِمَّا تَعُدُّونَ (5)

Commentary

The Greatness of the Quran, the Origin, and the Resurrection

Once again in this Surah, we encounter the "Disconnected Letters" (Alif-Lam-Mim), and this is the fifteenth time we find such letters at the beginning of Quranic Surahs.

At the beginning of Surahs al-Baqarah (Volume 1), Al-i-Imran (Volume 2), and al-A'raf (Volume 6), we discussed the various interpretations of these letters in detail. The discussion regarding the importance of the Quran immediately following these letters once again expresses the reality that "Alif-Lam-Mim" is an allusion to the greatness of the Quran and the magnificent manifestation of the Lord's power, who has created such a great and content-rich book—the eternal miracle of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله—from the simple letters of the "alphabet" that are available to everyone.

He says: "This is a Book revealed from the Lord of the Worlds, and there is no doubt in it" (تَنْزِيلُ الْكِتابِ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ). [1] This verse is actually an answer to two questions: it is as if first a question is asked about the content of this Heavenly Book, and in response, it says: its content is Truth and there is not the slightest place for doubt. Then a question arises about its Originator, and in response, it says this Book is from the "Lord of the Worlds."

It is also possible that the phrase "مِنْ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ" (from the Lord of the Worlds) is a proof for the phrase "لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ" (no doubt in it). It is as if someone asks: by what proof is this Book Truth and leaves no room for doubt? It says: by the proof that it is from the Lord of the Worlds, from whose existence every truth and reality flows.

Furthermore, the emphasis on the attribute "رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ" (Lord of the Worlds) among the attributes of Allah may be an indication that this Book is a collection of the wonders of the universe and an essence of the realities of the world of existence, because it is from the Lord of the Worlds.

It is also necessary to note that the Quran does not intend here to be content with a mere claim; rather, it wants to say that "what is manifest needs no explanation"—the content of this Book itself is a witness to its authenticity and correctness.

Then, referring to the slander that the polytheists and faithless hypocrites had repeatedly leveled against this great Heavenly Book, He says: "Or do they say: 'He has forged it'? Rather, it is the Truth from your Lord" (أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَراهُ بَلْ هُوَ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ). [1] In response to their baseless claim, it says: this is not a forgery, but a word of Truth from your Lord. And the proof of its truthfulness is manifest and apparent within itself.

Then, addressing the purpose of its revelation, He says: "The purpose was that you may warn a people to whom no warner has come before you, that perhaps they may be guided" (لِتُنْذِرَ قَوْماً ما أَتاهُمْ مِنْ نَذِيرٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ).

Although the call of the Prophet of Islam is both "glad tidings" and "warning," and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is more of a "bringer of glad tidings" (bashir) than a "warner" (nadhir), yet in the face of a straying and stubborn people, one must place more emphasis on "warning."

The phrase "هُوَ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ" (it is the Truth from your Lord) again indicates that the proof of its truthfulness is evident within itself.

And the phrase "لَعَلَّهُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ" (that perhaps they may be guided) indicates that the Quran provides the ground for guidance, but the final decision, in any case, rests with the human being himself.

Here, two questions arise:

  1. Who are these people to whom no warner had come before the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله?
  2. Furthermore, does the Quran not say: "And there was no nation but that a warner has passed within it" (Fatir: 24)?

In response to the first question, a group of commentators has said: it refers to the tribe of Quraysh, who had no divine warner before the Prophet of Islam.

Others have said: it refers to the period of fatrah (the interval between the mission of 'Isa عليه السلام and the appearance of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله).

However, neither of these two answers seems correct, because according to reasoned belief, the earth is never empty of a Divine Proof (Hujjah), and in every age and time, a Prophet or a successor (Wasi) of a Prophet has existed and exists among humans to establish the proof.

Therefore, it seems that "warner" (nadhir) here refers to a great Prophet who declares his call openly, accompanied by miracles, and in a vast and wide environment; and we know that such a warner had not risen in the Arabian Peninsula and among the tribes of "Makkah."

In response to the second question, it must be said: the phrase "وَ إِنْ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ إِلَّا خَلا فِيها نَذِيرٌ" means that every nation has had a warner, but it is not necessary for him to be personally present in every location. It is sufficient that the voice of the call of the great Divine Prophets reaches the ears of all the people of the world through their successors.

This statement is exactly like saying that every nation has had an Arch-Prophet (Ulul-'Azm) and possessed a Heavenly Book; the meaning is that the voice of this Prophet and his Heavenly Book reached all of that nation throughout history through his representatives and successors.

After stating the greatness of the Quran and the mission of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, He addresses another of the most important pillars of Islamic beliefs, namely Monotheism (Tawhid) and the negation of polytheism, saying: "Allah is He who created the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them in six days" (اللَّهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ). [1] We have repeatedly said that "six days" in these verses means "six periods," for we know that one of the meanings of "day" in everyday usage is "period," just as we say: one day the despotic regime ruled, and today the "consultative" system rules, while we know that despotic regimes ruled for thousands of years, yet they are referred to as a "day."

On the other hand, we know that various periods have passed over the heavens and the earth: One day, all the spheres of the solar system were a single molten mass. Another day, the planets separated from the sun and began to revolve around it. One day, the earth was entirely fire. And another day, it became cold and ready for the life of plants and animals; then living beings came into existence through various stages. (We have provided a detailed explanation of this meaning and the six periods in Volume 6, page 200 onwards, under verse 54 of Surah al-A'raf).

It is obvious that the infinite power of the Lord is sufficient to create this entire world in a short moment or less, but this gradual system can better express the greatness of Allah and His knowledge and governance in all stages.

For example, if a "fetus" completed its entire developmental period in one moment and was born, its wonders would remain hidden from human view. But when we see that every day and every week throughout these nine months it takes on a new and wonderful form and condition, and passes through various amazing stages one after another, we become better acquainted with the greatness of the Creator.

After the issue of creation, He addresses the issue of "Allah's Sovereignty" over the world of existence, saying: "Then He established Himself upon the Throne, and governed the entire world of existence" (ثُمَّ اسْتَوى‌ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ‌).

The word "Throne" ('Arsh), as we have said before, originally means high-pillared thrones and is usually a metaphor for power, just as in daily expressions we say: the pillars of so-and-so's throne collapsed, meaning his power was lost.

Therefore, Allah's establishing Himself upon the Throne is not in its physical sense—that Allah, like kings, has a throne and sits upon it—but rather it means that He is both the Creator of the world of existence and the Ruler over the entire universe. [1] And at the end of the verse, by referring to the issue of Monotheism in "Guardianship" (Wilayah) and "Intercession" (Shafa'ah), He completes the stages of Monotheism, saying: "You have besides Him no guardian and no intercessor" (ما لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَ لا شَفِيعٍ‌).

With this clear proof that the creation of the world is a sign of His sovereignty, and sovereignty is a proof for the Oneness of the Guardian, the Intercessor, and the One to be worshipped, why do you go astray and cling to idols? "Will you not then take heed?" (أَ فَلا تَتَذَكَّرُونَ‌).

In reality, the three stages of Monotheism reflected in the above verse are each considered a proof for the other: Monotheism in Creation is a proof for Monotheism in Sovereignty, and Monotheism in Sovereignty is a proof for Monotheism in Guardianship, Intercession, and Worship.

Here, a question has been raised by some commentators, the answer to which is not very complex: the last sentence says: besides Allah, you have no guardian or intercessor, and its meaning is that your Guardian and Intercessor is only Allah. Is it possible for someone to intercede with Himself?! This question can be answered in three ways:

  1. Considering that all intercessors must intercede with His permission—"Who is it that can intercede with Him except by His permission?" (al-Baqarah: 255)—it can be said that intercession, even if it comes from the Prophets and Divine Saints, still returns to His Holy Essence, whether the intercession is for the forgiveness of sins or for attaining Divine blessings. The witness to this statement is a verse with exactly the same theme as this verse at the beginning of Surah Yunus, where we read: "He governs the affair; there is no intercessor except after His permission" (Yunus: 3).

  2. Considering that when we supplicate at the threshold of the Lord, we resort to His attributes—we seek help from His All-Mercifulness and Compassion, from His Forgivingness and Pardoning, and from His Grace and Generosity—it is as if we make Him an intercessor with Himself, and we consider these attributes as intermediaries between ourselves and His Holy Essence, even though in reality His attributes are identical to His Essence. This is exactly what appears in the meaningful phrase of Ali عليه السلام in the Supplication of Kumayl: "And I seek intercession through You to Yourself!"

  3. The meaning of "intercessor" (shafi') here is helper and supporter, and we know that the helper, supporter, and victor is only Allah. The fact that some have taken intercession here to mean the creation and perfection of souls actually returns to this same meaning.

In the last verse under discussion, He first refers to the Monotheism of the Lord, then to the issue of "Resurrection," and in this way, the three branches of Monotheism mentioned in the previous verses (Monotheism in Creation, Sovereignty, and Worship) are completed here by mentioning "Monotheism in Lordship" (Rububiyyah), meaning the governance of the world of existence solely by Allah.

He says: "He governs the affair from the heaven to the earth" (يُدَبِّرُ الْأَمْرَ مِنَ السَّماءِ إِلَى الْأَرْضِ‌).

In other words, Allah has placed the entire world of existence, from the heaven to the earth, under the cover of His governance, and there is no governor in this world besides Him. [1] Then He adds: "Then it will ascend to Him in a Day the extent of which is a thousand years of those which you count" (ثُمَّ يَعْرُجُ إِلَيْهِ فِي يَوْمٍ كانَ مِقْدارُهُ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ مِمَّا تَعُدُّونَ‌).

The meaning of this "Day" is the Day of Resurrection.

To explain: Commentators have said much and given various possibilities in interpreting the above verse:

  1. Some have considered it an allusion to the "descending arc" and "ascending arc" of the world's governance in this very world.
  2. Some consider it an allusion to the Divine angels who traverse the distance from heaven to earth in five hundred years and return in the same amount of time, being occupied with the governance of this world by Allah's command.
  3. Others consider it an allusion to the periods of Divine governance in this world and believe that the various periods of governance are each a thousand years long, and in every thousand years, Allah commands His angels regarding the governance of the affair of the heaven and the earth, and after the end of this thousand-year period, another period begins.

These interpretations, while presenting vague and ambiguous points, have no specific evidence or witness from the verse itself or from other verses of the Quran.

In our opinion, the meaning of this verse, based on the evidence of other Quranic verses and also narrations regarding the interpretation of the verse, is something else.

And that is: Allah created this world, organized the heaven and the earth with a specific governance, and clothed humans and other living beings with the garment of life; but at the end of the world, He will fold up this governance. The sun will become dark, the stars will lose their light, and as the Quran says, He will roll up the heavens like a scroll, until they return to the state they were in before the expansion of this world: "The Day when We shall roll up the heaven like the rolling up of the scroll for writings; as We began the first creation, We shall repeat it" [1]. Following the folding up of this world, a new plan and a vaster world will be initiated; that is, after the end of this world, the other world begins.

This meaning also appears in other verses of the Quran, including verse 156 of Surah al-Baqarah: "Indeed we belong to Allah, and indeed to Him we are returning." And in Surah al-Rum, verse 27, it says: "And He it is who originates the creation, then He repeats it, and that is easier for Him."

And in verse 34 of Yunus, we read: "Say: Allah originates the creation, then He repeats it; so how are you deluded?"

Considering these expressions and other expressions that say all affairs eventually return to Allah—"And to Him the whole affair will be returned" (Surah Hud, verse 123)—it becomes clear that the verse under discussion also speaks of the beginning and end of the world and the establishment of the Day of Resurrection, which is sometimes referred to as the "descending arc" and "ascending arc."

Therefore, the concept of the verse is that "Allah governs the affair of this world from the heaven to the earth (it begins from the heaven and ends at the earth), then all of these return to Him on the Day of Resurrection."

In the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, under this very verse, we read: "It refers to the governance of the affairs that Allah governs, as well as the commands and prohibitions that have come in the Shari'ah and the deeds of all servants; all of these will be manifest on the Day of Resurrection, and the extent of that Day is equal to a thousand years of the years of this world." [1]

Here, the question arises that in verse 4 of Surah al-Ma'arij, regarding the length of the Day of Resurrection, we read: "The angels and the Spirit ascend to Him in a Day the extent of which is fifty thousand years." How can one reconcile the verse under discussion, which sets the extent at only one thousand years, with the verse of Surah al-Ma'arij?

The answer to this question is found in a Hadith narrated in Amali of Shaykh al-Tusi from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, who said:

"Indeed, in the Resurrection, there are fifty stations, each station being like a thousand years of those which you count." Then he recited this verse: "In a Day the extent of which is fifty thousand years."

Of course, these expressions are not inconsistent with the fact that the numbers one thousand and fifty thousand here may not be numerical counts, but rather each is for multiplication and expressing vastness; meaning that in the Resurrection there are fifty stations, and at each station, a person must stop for a very long time.

Key Points Misuse of the Verse "He Governs the Affair"

Some followers of fabricated sects in our time have used the above verse as a pretext to justify their sect, and through confusion and fallacy, they have tried to apply it to their purpose. Incidentally, with most of their missionaries that one encounters, one of the proofs they immediately cling to is this verse: "He governs the affair from the heaven to the earth..." They say:

The meaning of "affair" (amr) in this verse is "religion and creed," and "governance" (tadbir) means sending the religion, and "ascent" ('uruj) means the removal and abrogation of the religion! On this basis, every creed cannot live for more than a thousand years and must give its place to another creed. In this way, they say: we accept the Quran, but according to this very Quran, after a thousand years have passed, another creed will come!!

Now, as an impartial individual, we want to correctly examine and analyze the said verse to see if it has any connection to what they claim or not.

Setting aside that this meaning is so far from the concept of the verse that it would not occur to any unbiased reader.

Upon scrutiny, we see that applying the verse to what they say is not only inconsistent with the concept of the verse but also has clear problems from several aspects:

  1. Taking the word "affair" (amr) to mean religion and creed not only has no proof, but other verses of the Quran negate it, because in other verses, "affair" is used to mean the command of creation, such as: "His affair is only that when He intends a thing, He says to it: 'Be!' and it is" (Surah Ya-Sin, verse 82). In this verse and other verses like Surah al-Qamar verse 50, Surah al-Mu'minun verse 27, Surah al-A'raf verse 54, Surah Ibrahim verse 32, Surah al-Nahl verse 12, Surah al-Rum verse 25, Surah al-Jathiyah verse 12, and many other verses, "affair" is used in this same sense of a creative command (amr takwini), not in the sense of legislating a religion and creed. Basically, wherever there is talk of the heaven and the earth and creation and the like, "affair" is in this same sense.

  2. The word "governance" (tadbir) is also used in the context of creation and giving order to the state of the world of existence, not in the sense of revealing a creed. Therefore, we see that in other verses of the Quran (verses interpret one another), the word "governance" is never used regarding religion and creed; rather, the words "legislation" (tashri'), "revelation" (tanzil), or "sending down" (inzal) are used. "He has ordained (shara'a) for you of religion what He enjoined upon Nuh" (al-Shura: 13). "And whoever does not judge by what Allah has revealed (anzala), those are the disbelievers" (al-Ma'idah: 44). "He has revealed (nazzala) to you the Book in truth, confirming what was before it" (Al-i-Imran: 3).

  3. The verses before and after the "verse under discussion" relate to the creation of the world, not to the legislation of religions. In the "previous" verse, the talk was about the creation of the heavens and the earth in six days (or six periods), and in the "following" verses, the talk is about the creation of man. It is obvious that the harmony of the verses requires that this verse, which is located in the middle of the "creation" verses, also relates to the issue of creation and the governance of the affair of creation. Therefore, if we study the books of commentary written hundreds of years ago, we see that although various possibilities have been given in the interpretation of this verse, no one has suggested that the verse relates to the legislation of religions. For example, in Majma' al-Bayan, which is among the most famous Islamic commentaries and whose author lived in the sixth century AH, although various views are mentioned in the interpretation of the above verse, he has not narrated from any Islamic scholar a view stating that the verse relates to the legislation of religions.

  4. The word "ascent" ('uruj) means "climbing and going up," not the abrogation and disappearance of religions. Nowhere in the Quran is "ascent" seen to mean "abrogation" (this word is mentioned in five verses of the Quran and in no case does it have this meaning). Rather, regarding religions, the words "abrogation" (naskh), "replacement" (tabdil), and the like are used. Basically, religions and Heavenly Books are not things that, for example, like human souls, fly to the heaven with angels after the end of their life; rather, abrogated faiths remain on this very earth but have lost their validity in some matters, while their principles remain in force. In short, the word "ascent," besides not being used anywhere in the Holy Quran to mean the abrogation of religions, is fundamentally incompatible with the concept of the abrogation of religions, because abrogated religions have no ascent to the heaven.

  5. In addition to all this, this meaning does not correspond to objective reality at all, because the interval between past religions was in no case exactly one thousand years! For example, the interval between the appearance of Prophet Musa عليه السلام and Prophet 'Isa عليه السلام was more than 1500 years, and the interval between Prophet 'Isa عليه السلام and the appearance of the great Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله was less than 600 years! As you observe, neither of these two matches the thousand years they claim; rather, there is a great difference. The interval between the appearance of Nuh عليه السلام, who was one of the Arch-Prophets and the founder of a specific creed and Shari'ah, and the idol-breaking hero Ibrahim عليه السلام, who was another Prophet with a Shari'ah, is written as more than 1600 years, and the interval between Ibrahim عليه السلام and Musa عليه السلام as less than 500 years. From this subject, we conclude that even as a single example, the interval of one of the past creeds and religions with the faith after it was not a thousand years.

  6. Beyond all this, the claim of Sayyid Ali Muhammad Bab, for whose sake all these improper justifications have been endured, does not fit this calculation at all. By their own admission, his birth was in the year 1235 and the start of his claim was in the year 1260 AH. Considering that the start of the Prophet of Islam's صلى الله عليه وآله call was 13 years before the Hijrah, the interval between these two becomes 1273 years, meaning it has an extra "273" years! Now, with what plan should we submerge this "273" and how should we ignore this large number? One must ask them!

  7. Furthermore, suppose we set aside all these six objections and ignore these clear analyses and call upon reason to judge. Suppose we, instead of the Quran, wanted to clarify the duty of future generations toward new claimants of prophethood and say: "After a thousand years have passed, expect a new prophet." Was the way to say it in the form mentioned in the said verse, such that for twelve or thirteen centuries, not a single scholar or non-scholar had the slightest knowledge of the verse's meaning, and only after 1273 years have passed, a group uncovers it as a "new discovery" which is only accepted by themselves and not others? Would it not have been more rational that instead of this sentence, it was said: "I give you glad tidings that after a thousand years, a prophet by this name will appear," just as 'Isa عليه السلام said regarding the Prophet of Islam: "And bringing glad tidings of a messenger who will come after me, whose name is Ahmad" (Surah al-Saff, verse 6). In any case, perhaps this issue did not need to be discussed to the extent we have, but to clarify for the young Muslim generation regarding the traps prepared by global colonialism and the sects fabricated to weaken the front of Islam, we had no choice but to let them know a corner of their logic and calculate the rest themselves.

who perfected everything that He created and commenced man’s creation from clay. (7)

Commentary

The Wondrous Stages of Human Creation

The verses under discussion first point to and emphasize the monotheistic themes mentioned in the preceding verses, which were summarized in four stages (Oneness of Creation, Sovereignty, Guardianship, and Lordship). It states: "The One described with these attributes is the God who is aware of the hidden and the manifest, and He is the All-Mighty, the Merciful" (ذلِكَ عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ وَ الشَّهادَةِ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ).

It is evident that He who intends to manage the affairs of the heavens and the earth, to be Sovereign over them, and to hold the station of Guardianship, Intercession, and Creation, must be aware of everything—both the hidden and the manifest. Without such expansive knowledge and awareness, none of these matters would be possible.

At the same time, such a Being must be "Al-Aziz" (the All-Mighty and Invincible) so that He may perform these momentous tasks. Yet, His might and power are not coupled with harshness, but rather with Mercy and Grace!

The next verse points to the "Best System" of creation in general, and serves as a prelude to explaining the creation of man and his stages of evolution in particular. It says: "He is the One who made beautiful everything He created" (الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ خَلَقَهُ). He gave to every thing that which it required; in other words, He established the edifice of the great palace of creation upon the "Best System"—that is, upon such an order that nothing more perfect could be imagined.

He created harmony and connection among all beings, and granted to each what they requested through the "language of their state" (zaban-e hal).

If we look at the existence of a human being and consider each of his bodily systems, we see that in terms of structure, volume, the state of the cells, and their mode of operation, they are created exactly in a way that allows them to perform their duty in the best possible manner. Simultaneously, He has placed such a connection between the organs that all, without exception, influence one another and are influenced by one another.

This exact meaning governs the macro-world with its highly diverse creatures, especially in the world of living organisms with their vastly different systems.

In short: The Giver who gave fragrance to the rose and life to the clay, To everyone, His Wisdom gave what He deemed worthy!

Yes, it is He who grants various delightful scents to different flowers, and it is He who gives spirit and life to dust and clay, fashioning from it a free and intelligent human being. From this same dark earth, He sometimes creates various flowers, sometimes man, and sometimes other types of beings; even the earth itself possesses, within its own limits, what it ought to have.

Similar to this is what we read in Surah Ta-Ha, verse 50, quoted from Moses and Aaron: رَبُّنَا الَّذِي أَعْطى‌ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدى‌: "Our Lord is He who gave to each thing its form and then guided it."

Here, a question arises regarding the creation of evils and pests and how they reconcile with the "Best System of the world," which we will examine, God willing, in the "Key Points" section.

The Quran then moves from these "External" (afaqi) signs to "Internal" (anfusi) discussions. Just as it spoke of several branches of Monotheism in the external signs, here it speaks of several great blessings regarding human beings.

First, it says: "Allah began the creation of man from clay" (وَ بَدَأَ خَلْقَ الْإِنْسانِ مِنْ طِينٍ).

This is both to demonstrate His greatness and power—that He created such an outstanding creature from such a simple and lowly substance, fashioning that "delightful" form from "water and clay"—and to warn this human being: where have you come from and where are you going?

It is clear that this verse speaks of the creation of "Adam," not all humans, because the continuation of his progeny is discussed in the following verse. The apparent meaning of this verse is clear evidence for the independent creation of man and the rejection of the hypothesis of the "evolution of species" (at least regarding the human species).

Although some have sought to interpret this verse in a way that aligns with the hypothesis of evolution—arguing that human creation traces back to lower species, which ultimately trace back to water and clay—the apparent wording of the verse suggests that there were no other species (countless ones at that) between "Adam" and "clay." Rather, the creation of man took place directly from clay.

Of course, the Quran has not spoken regarding other types of living creatures.

This meaning becomes clearer when considering verse 59 of Surah Al-Imran, where it says: إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسى‌ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ كَمَثَلِ آدَمَ خَلَقَهُ مِنْ تُرابٍ: "The creation of Jesus without a father is not a strange thing; it is like the creation of Adam, whom He created from dust."

And in Surah Al-Hijr, verse 26, He says: وَ لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانِ مِنْ صَلْصالٍ مِنْ حَمَإٍ مَسْنُونٍ: "We created man from sounding clay, from mud molded into shape."

From the sum of these verses, it is inferred that the creation of Adam occurred as an independent creation from dust and clay. We know that the hypothesis of the evolution of species has never become a definitive scientific fact such that we would need to interpret these verses otherwise due to a contradiction. In other words, as long as there is no clear evidence contrary to the apparent meaning of the verses, they must be applied according to their literal sense, and such is the case regarding the independent creation of Adam.

The next verse refers to the creation of the human race and how the children of Adam are born in subsequent stages, saying: "Then He made his progeny from an extract of a humble and insignificant fluid" (ثُمَّ جَعَلَ نَسْلَهُ مِنْ سُلالَةٍ مِنْ ماءٍ مَهِينٍ). "Jal" (made) here means creation, and "nasl" (progeny) refers to children and grandchildren through all stages.

"Sulalah" originally means an extract or the pure essence of something. What is meant here is the human sperm (nutfah), which is in fact the essence of his entire being and the starting point of life and the source of the birth of children and the continuation of the race.

This fluid, which is apparently worthless and insignificant, is extremely delicate, extraordinarily precise, and complex in terms of its structure and the living cells floating within it, as well as the specific composition of the liquid. It is considered one of the signs of the Lord's greatness, knowledge, and power. The word "mahin," which means weak, humble, and insignificant, refers to its outward appearance; otherwise, it is among the most mysterious of entities.

The next verse refers to the complex stages of human development in the womb, as well as the stages Adam underwent during his creation from dust. It says: "Then He proportioned him" (ثُمَّ سَوَّاهُ). "And breathed into him of His spirit!" (وَ نَفَخَ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِهِ). "And He made for you hearing, and sight, and hearts" (وَ جَعَلَ لَكُمُ السَّمْعَ وَ الْأَبْصارَ وَ الْأَفْئِدَةَ). "But little is the thanks you give" (قَلِيلًا ما تَشْكُرُونَ).

"Sawwahu" is derived from "taswiyah," meaning to complete or perfect. This refers to the sum of the stages a human undergoes from the time he is a sperm until the stage where all his bodily organs become manifest, as well as the stages Adam traversed from his creation from dust until the breathing of the spirit. [^1] The expression "nafkh" (breathing/blowing) is a metaphor for the soul entering the human body, as if likened to air and breathing, though it is neither this nor that.

If it is asked: the human sperm is a living entity from the moment it is placed in the womb and even before that, so what does "breathing of the spirit" mean?

In response, we say: at the beginning when the sperm is conceived, it possesses only a type of "vegetative life"—meaning it only has nutrition and growth. However, there is no sign of "animal life" (sensation and movement) or "human life" (the power of perception).

But the evolution of the sperm in the womb reaches a stage where it begins to move, and gradually other human faculties come alive within it. This is the very stage that the Quran describes as the "breathing of the spirit."

The attribution of "Spirit" to "God" is what is known as an "honorific attribution" (idafah tashrifiyyah). That is, a highly valuable and noble spirit, worthy of being called the Spirit of God, was breathed into man. This expresses the reality that although man, from a "material dimension," is from "dark dust" or "insignificant water," from a "spiritual and soul dimension," he carries the "Divine Spirit."

One side of his existence ends in dust, and the other side reaches the Throne of the Lord. He is a "wondrous mixture" fashioned "from angel and from animal!" Because of possessing these two dimensions, his ascending and descending arcs, and his perfection and degradation, are extraordinarily vast. [^1] In the final stage, which is considered the fifth stage of human creation, the Quran mentions the blessings of hearing, sight, and the heart. Of course, the intention here is not the physical creation of these organs, as that occurs before the breathing of the spirit; rather, the intention is the sense of hearing, sight, and understanding and intellect.

If, out of all the "external" and "internal" senses, only these three are emphasized, it is because the most important external senses that establish a powerful connection between man and the outside world are hearing and sight. Hearing perceives sounds, and education and training are specifically carried out through it. Sight is the means of seeing the external world and the various scenes of this universe.

The power of intellect and reason is also the most important internal sense of man, or in other words, the ruler of human existence.

It is interesting that "af'idah" is the plural of "fu'ad," meaning heart, but it has a more delicate concept. This word is usually used in a context where there is "burning" and "ripeness" (intensity of feeling and maturity).

In this way, Allah has described the most important tools of cognition in the "outward" and "inward" of human existence in this verse. This is because human knowledge is either obtained through "experience," for which the tools are the eyes and ears, or through "rational analysis and reasoning," for which the tool is the intellect and reason, expressed in the Quran as "af'idah." Even the perceptions that occur through revelation or inspiration and intuition upon the human heart are still through these "af'idah."

If these tools of cognition were taken away from man, his existential value would drop to the level of a handful of stone and dust. For this reason, at the end of the verse, He draws human attention to the issue of gratitude for these great blessings and says: "Little is the thanks you give," implying that no matter how much you thank Him for these great blessings, it is still little!

Key Point: How Adam Was Created from Dust

Although various verses of the Quran sometimes speak of the creation of man from "clay" (as in the verses above and in the story of Adam and Iblis in Surah Al-Isra, verse 61: "All the angels prostrated to Adam except Iblis; he said: 'Shall I prostrate to one whom You created from clay?'"), and sometimes speak of creation from "water" (such as: "And We made from water every living thing" - Al-Anbiya, 30), it is clear that all of these refer to the same reality. Even where it speaks of the creation of Adam from "turab" (dust), such as: "Indeed, the likeness of Jesus with Allah is as the likeness of Adam; He created him from dust" (Al-Imran, 59), the meaning is dust mixed with water, which is clay.

From this, two points become clear:

  1. Those who have suggested that the creation of man from dust means that human beings feed on plants—directly or indirectly—and plants all come from the soil, are incorrect. This is because the verses of the Quran interpret one another, and the verses under discussion, by the evidence of other Quranic verses, refer to the creation of the "person of Adam" who was created from dust.

  2. All these verses are evidence for the rejection of the hypothesis of evolution (at least in the case of humans) and that the human species, which traces back to "Adam," has an independent creation.

Those who have imagined that the verses of creation from dust refer to the human species tracing back through thousands of intermediaries to single-celled organisms—which, according to recent hypotheses, emerged from the sludge at the edge of oceans—and that "Adam" himself was an individual chosen from among the human species and did not have an independent creation (rather, his distinction was his special attributes), this is in no way compatible with the apparent meanings of the Quranic verses.

We emphasize again that the issue of the evolution of species is not a definitive scientific law; it is only a hypothesis. Something whose roots are hidden millions of years ago and is certainly not subject to sensory experiment and observation cannot be placed in the rank of established scientific laws. Rather, it is a hypothesis created to justify the phenomenon of the diversity of species, and its value is no more than providing an estimated justification for phenomena. We know that hypotheses are constantly in a state of change and flux, yielding the field to new hypotheses.

Therefore, one can never rely on them in philosophical matters that require definitive foundations.

A detailed explanation regarding the foundations of the hypothesis of evolution and its shortcomings was provided in Volume 11, page 81 onwards, under the title "The Quran and the Creation of Man" following verse 28 of Surah Al-Hijr.

At the end of this discussion, we find it necessary to remind that the hypothesis of evolution has no connection to the issue of "Monotheism and the knowledge of God," and is not considered a reason for denying the metaphysical world. This is because monotheistic belief says: the world was created by Allah, and He granted all properties to beings, and in all stages, grace is bestowed upon them by Allah. A believer in the "fixity of species" can accept this meaning, just as a believer in the "evolution of species" can. The only problem the hypothesis of evolution faces is that it is incompatible with the description the Quran provides for the creation of Adam and how He mentions his creation from dust and clay.

Therefore, we reject the hypothesis of evolution only for this reason, not because of its opposition to the issue of Monotheism—this is from an interpretative perspective.

As for the scientific perspective (natural sciences), we reject the hypothesis of evolution, as mentioned, because there is no definitive evidence for its proof.

[^1]: It is noteworthy that some consider this verse only as a reference to the stages of embryonic development, while others have suggested it refers only to the stages of Adam's development after creation from dust (since the same expressions appear in other verses regarding Adam's creation). However, there is no obstacle to it referring to both, as both the creation of Adam from dust and his progeny from the water of sperm have traversed and continue to traverse these stages.
