Commentary

Regret and the Request to Return

These verses begin with an eloquent discussion regarding the "Resurrection," then recount the state of the "criminals" in the other world. Collectively, they serve as a completion to the previous discussions which centered around the "Origin" (Creation), for we know that the discussion of the "Origin" and the "Resurrection" in the Holy Quran are often coupled together.

First, It says: "And they said, 'When we have been lost within the earth, will we indeed be recreated in a new creation?'" (وَ قالُوا أَ إِذا ضَلَلْنا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَ إِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ).

The expression "lost within the earth" (ضَلَلْنا فِي الْأَرْضِ) refers to the fact that after death, man becomes dust like any other dust, and every particle of him is scattered to some corner by natural or unnatural factors, until it seems nothing remains of him to be brought back on the Day of Resurrection.

However, in reality, they do not deny Allah's power over this act, "but they are disbelievers in the meeting with their Lord" (بَلْ هُمْ بِلِقاءِ رَبِّهِمْ كافِرُونَ).

They wish to deny the stage of meeting the Lord—which is the stage of accounting, reckoning, reward, and punishment—so that they may subsequently find freedom of action to do whatever they desire!

In fact, this verse bears a great resemblance to the opening verses of Surah Al-Qiyamah, where it says: "Does man think that We will not assemble his bones? Yes, [We are] Able [even] to proportion his fingertips. But man desires to continue in sin ahead of him. He asks, 'When is the Day of Resurrection?'" (Surah Al-Qiyamah, 3-6).

Therefore, they are not lacking in terms of reasoning; rather, their lusts have cast a veil over their hearts, and their evil intentions prevent them from accepting the issue of Resurrection. Otherwise, the same God who granted a piece of magnet the property to attract tiny particles of iron lost amidst tons of earth with a single sweep, can surely create such mutual attraction among the particles of a human body.

Who can deny that the water present in a human body (and most of the human body is water), as well as its nutrients—each part of which, for example, a thousand years ago was scattered in some corner of the world, every drop in an ocean and every particle in a different climate—were gathered through clouds, rain, and other natural factors to eventually form a human being? What is there to wonder at if, after decomposing and returning to their original state, they should once again gather and join together?

The next verse provides their answer through another path, saying: Do not imagine that your personality is defined by this physical body of yours; rather, the foundation of your personality is formed by your soul, and it is preserved. "Say, 'The angel of death who has been entrusted with you will take you. Then to your Lord you will be returned'" (قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ ثُمَّ إِلى‌ رَبِّكُمْ تُرْجَعُونَ).

Considering the concept of "يتوفاكم" which comes from the root "توفى" (on the pattern of tasaddi) meaning "to take back in full," death does not mean annihilation or non-existence. Rather, it is a type of seizure and reception by the angels regarding the human soul, which constitutes the most fundamental part of human existence.

It is true that the Quran speaks of physical resurrection and considers the return of both the soul and the material body in the Resurrection as certain, but the aim of the above verse is to express the reality that the foundation of human personality is not these material parts that occupy all your thoughts, but rather that spiritual essence which came from God and returns to Him.

In summary, it can be said that the two verses above answer the deniers of Resurrection as follows: If your problem is the scattering of physical parts, you yourselves accept God's power and do not deny it; and if the problem is the dissolution and destruction of human personality due to this scattering, that too is incorrect, because the basis of human personality rests upon the soul.

This objection is not unlike the famous "Eater and Eaten" (Akil wa Ma'kul) paradox, just as the answer to it in both cases is similar. [1] Additionally, it is necessary to note that in some verses of the Quran, "توفى" (taking the soul) is attributed to Allah: "Allah takes the souls at the time of their death" (Az-Zumar, 42). In some, it is attributed to a group of angels: "...those whom the angels take in death while they are wronging themselves..." (An-Nahl, 28). And in the verses under discussion, the taking of souls is attributed to the "Angel of Death" (Malak al-Mawt).

However, there is no contradiction between these expressions. "Angel of Death" can have a generic meaning referring to all such angels, or it refers to their chief and leader. And since they all take souls by the command of Allah, it is also attributed to Allah.

Then, the verse depicts the state of these criminal disbelievers and deniers of Resurrection, who, upon witnessing the various scenes of the Day of Judgment, become deeply regretful and remorseful for their past, saying: "If you could but see when the criminals are hanging their heads before their Lord, [saying], 'Our Lord, we have seen and heard, so return us [to the world]; we will work righteousness. Indeed, we are [now] certain'" (you would be filled with wonder) (وَ لَوْ تَرى‌ إِذِ الْمُجْرِمُونَ ناكِسُوا رُؤُسِهِمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ رَبَّنا أَبْصَرْنا وَ سَمِعْنا فَارْجِعْنا نَعْمَلْ صالِحاً إِنَّا مُوقِنُونَ). 2 Truly, you would wonder: are these individuals, with heads bowed in regret and remorse, the same arrogant, rebellious, and stubborn ones who in the world would not bow before any truth? But now that they see the scenes of Resurrection and reach the stage of direct witnessing, they completely change their stance. Yet, this change of stance and awakening is fleeting; according to other verses of the Quran, if they were returned to this world, they would continue in their same old ways (Al-An'am, verse 28). "ناكس" (Nakis) is from the root "نكس" (on the pattern of aks) meaning to turn something upside down, and here it means hanging the head down.

Placing "ابصرنا" (we have seen) before "سمعنا" (we have heard) is because on the Day of Resurrection, man first encounters the scenes and then hears the divine interrogation and the words of His angels.

Furthermore, from what we have said, it becomes clear that the "criminals" intended here are the disbelievers, and specifically the deniers of Resurrection.

In any case, this is not the first time we encounter this issue in the verses of the Quran—that criminals, upon witnessing the results of their deeds and the effects of divine punishment, become deeply regretful and request to return to the world, while according to divine law, such a return is impossible, just as the return of a newborn to the mother's womb or a fruit separated from the tree back to the tree is impossible.

It is also noteworthy that the only request of the criminals is to return to the world to perform righteous deeds. This clearly shows that the only capital for salvation in the Resurrection is righteous deeds—deeds performed from a pure heart filled with faith and with sincerity of intention.

And since all this insistence and emphasis on accepting faith might create the illusion that Allah is unable to radiate the light of faith into their hearts, the next verse adds: "And if We had willed, We could have given every soul its guidance [by compulsion]" (وَ لَوْ شِئْنا لَآتَيْنا كُلَّ نَفْسٍ هُداها).

Certainly, We have such power, but faith that is realized through compulsion and obligation has little value. Our will has been directed toward granting the human race the honor of being "autonomous" (possessing free will) and traversing the path of perfection on their own feet.

Therefore, at the end of the verse, He says: "I have left them free, but the word from Me has come true: 'I will surely fill Hell with the jinn and people all together'" (وَ لكِنْ حَقَّ الْقَوْلُ مِنِّي لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنَ الْجِنَّةِ وَ النَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ).

Yes, they have traversed this path by their own poor choice and deserve punishment, and We have the firm resolve to fill Hell with them.

Given what was said above, and considering hundreds of Quranic verses that regard man as a being with free will and volition, obligated by duties and responsible for his actions, and capable of being guided by prophets and through self-purification, any illusion that the above verse is evidence for predestination (Jabr)—as Fakhr al-Razi and his ilk imagined—is dismissed.

It is possible that the above decisive and crushing sentence is an indication that you should not imagine that the Mercy and Compassion of Allah prevent the punishment of tainted and oppressive criminals. Do not be deceived by the verses of mercy and consider yourselves exempt from divine punishment, for His mercy has its place and His wrath has its place.

He will certainly—especially considering the Lam of oath in the phrase "لأملأن" and the Nun of emphasis at its end—fulfill this promise of His and fill Hell with these dwellers of Hell, for if He did not, it would be contrary to Wisdom.

Therefore, in the next verse, He says: We will say to these dwellers of Hell: "So taste [the punishment] because you forgot the meeting of this, your Day, and indeed, We [too] have forgotten you!" (فَذُوقُوا بِما نَسِيتُمْ لقاءَ يَوْمِكُمْ هذا إِنَّا نَسِيناكُمْ). "And taste the punishment of eternity for what you used to do" (وَ ذُوقُوا عَذابَ الْخُلْدِ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ).

Once again, it is inferred from this verse that forgetting the court of justice of the Resurrection is the primary source of human misfortunes, for in that state, one sees oneself as free to break laws and commit oppressions. It is also clearly evident from this verse that eternal punishment is in return for the deeds that man performs and nothing else. [3] Additionally, the meaning of the Lord "forgetting" His servants is indifference and the withdrawal of support and aid; otherwise, the entire universe is always present before the Lord, and forgetfulness regarding Him is meaningless.

Key Points

1- The Independence and Primacy of the Soul

The first of the above verses, which indicates the taking of souls by the Angel of Death, is among the proofs for the independence of the human soul.

For the expression "توفى," which is to receive and seize in full, shows that the soul remains after separation from the body and is not destroyed. Fundamentally, referring to the human in the above verse as "soul" or "self" is another witness to this meaning.

According to the view of materialists, the soul is nothing but the "physicochemical" properties of brain cells that are destroyed with the annihilation of the body, much like the cessation of a clock's hands' movement after the clock is destroyed.

According to this view, the soul is not something that preserves human personality; rather, it is a part of the properties of his body that vanishes with the decomposition of the body.

We have several philosophical proofs for the primacy and independence of the soul, some of which we provided under verse 85 of Surah Al-Isra (Volume 12, page 254 onwards). The purpose here was to state the textual evidence for this subject, and the above verse is considered among those verses indicating this meaning.

2- The Angel of Death (Malak al-Mawt)

It is understood from various verses of the Holy Quran that Allah manages the affairs of this world through a group of angels, as He says in verse 5 of Surah An-Nazi'at: "فَالْمُدَبِّراتِ أَمْراً": "And those who regulate the matter [by command of Allah]."

We know that the divine tradition is to implement His will through means and causes.

And a group of these angels are the angels of taking souls, who are mentioned in verses 28 and 33 of Surah An-Nahl and some other verses of the Quran, and at their head stands the "Angel of Death."

Many hadiths have been narrated in this regard, some of which are necessary to mention for several reasons:

1- In a hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that he said: "Illnesses and pains are all the couriers of death and the messengers of death! When the appointed time arrives, the Angel of Death comes himself and says: 'O servant! How many reports after reports? How many messengers after messengers? How many couriers after couriers? I am the report after which there is no report!'"

"Diseases and pains are all couriers of death and his messengers. When a person's life comes to an end, the Angel of Death comes (the person is terrified at seeing the Angel of Death and thinks it is without warning, but) he says: 'O servant of Allah, how many reports after reports, messengers after messengers, and couriers after couriers did I send to you? But I am the final report, and after me, there is no report!' Then he says: 'Respond to the call of your Lord, whether willingly or unwillingly!'" And when the Angel of Death takes his soul, the cries of the relatives rise. He calls out: "Upon whom do you cry out? And for whom do you weep? By Allah, I have not wronged his appointed time, nor have I consumed his provision; rather, his Lord called him."

"If the weeper must weep, let him weep for himself! I shall return to you again and again until I leave not one of you remaining!" [4] Read this soul-shaking hadith once more, for many truths are hidden within it.

2- In another hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read that the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله came to the house of a man from the Ansar to visit him. He saw the Angel of Death at his head. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Treat this friend of mine with leniency and kindness, for he is a man of faith." He replied: "O Muhammad! Glad tidings to you, for I am affectionate toward all believers. And know, O Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, that when I take the soul of some of the children of Adam, his family cries out; I stand by the house and say: 'I have no sin (his life had ended); I shall return to you again and again! Be alert, be alert!'" Then he adds: "Allah has not created any human from the dwellers of cities or deserts, houses or tents, on land or sea, except that I examine them every day and night five times, until I know their young and old better than they know themselves!" [5]

Other narrations with the same theme have appeared in various Islamic sources. Studying them collectively is a warning for all humans to know that there is not a great distance between them and death! In a short moment, everything may end.

In such a state, is it appropriate for man to be deceived by the glitz and glamour of this world, become tainted with various types of oppression and sin, and remain heedless of the end of the matter?

[1]: For more explanation regarding the "Eater and Eaten" paradox and its detailed answer, refer to Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 260 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

[3]: Regarding the "Philosophy of Eternity and Everlasting Punishment," we had a detailed discussion in Volume 9, page 239 onwards (under verse 107 of Surah Hud). [4]: Majma' al-Bayan under the verse discussed, and Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 4, p. 225. [5]: Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur, as quoted in Al-Mizan, vol. 16, p. 268.

Ayahs

إِنَّمَا يُؤْمِنُ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا ٱلَّذِينَ إِذَا ذُكِّرُوا۟ بِهَا خَرُّوا۟ سُجَّدًا وَسَبَّحُوا۟ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَهُمْ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ ۩ (١٥)
Only those believe in Our signs who, when they are reminded of them, fall down in prostration and celebrate the praise of their Lord, and they are not arrogant. (15)
تَتَجَافَىٰ جُنُوبُهُمْ عَنِ ٱلْمَضَاجِعِ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُمْ خَوْفًا وَطَمَعًا وَمِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ يُنفِقُونَ (١٦)
Their sides vacate their beds to supplicate their Lord in fear and hope, and they spend out of what We have provided them. (16)
فَلَا تَعْلَمُ نَفْسٌ مَّآ أُخْفِىَ لَهُم مِّن قُرَّةِ أَعْيُنٍ جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٧)
No one knows what delights have been kept hidden for them [in the Hereafter] as a reward for what they used to do. (17)
أَفَمَن كَانَ مُؤْمِنًا كَمَن كَانَ فَاسِقًا ۚ لَّا يَسْتَوُۥنَ (١٨)
Is someone who is faithful like someone who is a transgressor? They are not equal. (18)
أَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فَلَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتُ ٱلْمَأْوَىٰ نُزُلًۢا بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٩)
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, for them will be the gardens of the Abode—a hospitality for what they used to do. (19)
وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ فَسَقُوا۟ فَمَأْوَىٰهُمُ ٱلنَّارُ ۖ كُلَّمَآ أَرَادُوٓا۟ أَن يَخْرُجُوا۟ مِنْهَآ أُعِيدُوا۟ فِيهَا وَقِيلَ لَهُمْ ذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابَ ٱلنَّارِ ٱلَّذِى كُنتُم بِهِۦ تُكَذِّبُونَ (٢٠)
As for those who have transgressed, their refuge will be the Fire. Whenever they seek to leave it, they will be turned back into it and told: ‘Taste the punishment of the Fire which you used to deny.’ (20)

Commentary

Great Rewards That No One Knows!

We know that the method of the Quran is to present many truths in comparison with one another, so that they become fully understood and heart-settling.

Here, too, after the description given regarding the criminals and disbelievers in the preceding verses, it addresses the prominent qualities of the true believers. It succinctly expresses their ideological principles and practical programs within two verses by mentioning eight attributes [^1]. First, It says: "Only those believe in Our verses who, when they are reminded of them, fall down in prostration and hymn the praise of their Lord, and they are not arrogant" (إِنَّما يُؤْمِنُ بِآياتِنَا الَّذِينَ إِذا ذُكِّرُوا بِها خَرُّوا سُجَّداً وَ سَبَّحُوا بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَ هُمْ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ).

The use of the term "Innama" (إِنَّما), which is usually for restriction (hasr), highlights the point that whenever someone claims to have faith but does not possess the characteristics mentioned in these verses, he is not among the ranks of the true believers; he is a person of weak faith who cannot be counted as such.

In this verse, four of their attributes are described:

1- As soon as they hear the Divine verses, they fall into prostration. The expression "kharru" (خَرُّوا) instead of "sajadu" (سجدوا) points to a subtle nuance: this group of awake-hearted believers, upon hearing the verses of the Quran, become so enamored and captivated by the words of the Lord that they involuntarily fall into prostration, losing their hearts and souls in this path [^2]. Indeed, their first characteristic is that burning love and fiery affection for the speech of their Beloved and Deity.

[^1]: It should be noted that the first verse is the first verse of "obligatory prostration" (Sajdah Wajib) in the Holy Quran; if someone recites it in full or hears it from another, it is obligatory to prostrate. Of course, ablution (wudu) is not mandatory for it, but the obligatory precaution is to place the forehead on something upon which prostration is valid.

[^2]: Raghib in "Al-Mufradat" says: "Kharru" is originally from the root "kharir," which means the sound of water and the like falling from a height. Using this expression regarding those prostrating indicates that at the very moment they fall to the ground for prostration, their voices are raised in glorification (tasbih).

This same characteristic is mentioned in some other verses of the Quran as one of the most prominent attributes of the Prophets. As Allah says regarding a group of the great Prophets: إِذا تُتْلى‌ عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُ الرَّحْمنِ خَرُّوا سُجَّداً وَ بُكِيًّا: "When the verses of the Most Merciful were recited to them, they fell down prostrating and weeping [in yearning]" (Surah Maryam, Verse 58).

Although "verses" (ayat) is mentioned here in an absolute sense, it is evident that it primarily refers to those verses that call toward Monotheism (Tawhid) and the struggle against polytheism (shirk).

2 and 3- Their second and third signs are "Tasbih" (glorification) and "Hamd" (praise) of the Lord. On one hand, they deem Allah free and pure from all defects, and on the other, they praise and extol Him for His attributes of perfection and beauty.

4- Their other description is humility and the abandonment of any kind of arrogance, for pride and ego are the first rungs on the ladder of disbelief and lack of faith, while humility before the Truth and reality is the first step of faith! Those who walk the path of pride and self-centeredness neither prostrate before God, nor perform His glorification and praise, nor recognize the rights of His servants. They have a great idol, and that is themselves!

Then, referring to their other attributes, It says: "Their sides forsake their beds" in the middle of the night; they rise and turn toward the threshold of God, engaging in intimate prayer and supplication with Him (تَتَجافى‌ جُنُوبُهُمْ عَنِ الْمَضاجِعِ) [^1].

[^1]: "Tatajafa" is from the root "jafa," which originally means lifting and distancing. "Junub" is the plural of "janb," meaning side, and "madaji'" is the plural of "madja'," meaning bed. The distancing of the side from the bed is a metaphor for rising from sleep and engaging in the worship of the Lord in the depth of the night.

Indeed, at the time when the eyes of the heedless are in sleep, they wake for a portion of the night. At that time when the ordinary routines of life are suspended, mental distractions reach their minimum, and tranquility and silence have enveloped everywhere—and the risk of polluting worship with hypocrisy (riya) is at its lowest—in short, the best conditions for the presence of the heart are provided. They turn with their whole being toward the threshold of the Deity, rub their foreheads upon the doorstep of the Beloved, and share whatever they have in their hearts with Him. They live by His remembrance and keep the cup of their hearts overflowing and brimming with His love.

Then It adds: "They call upon their Lord in fear and hope" (يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُمْ خَوْفاً وَ طَمَعاً).

Yes, two other descriptions of theirs are "fear" (khawf) and "hope" (raja).

They neither feel secure from His wrath and punishment, nor do they despair of His mercy. The balance of this fear and hope, which guarantees their perfection and progress on the path of God, always reigns within their being.

For the dominance of fear over hope leads man to despair and weakness. And the dominance of hope and desire leads man to pride and heedlessness, and both of these are enemies of man's evolutionary movement in his journey toward God.

The last and eighth characteristic of theirs is that: "And they spend out of what We have provided them" (وَ مِمَّا رَزَقْناهُمْ يُنْفِقُونَ).

They do not only give of their wealth to the needy; they do not withhold their knowledge, strength, power, sound judgment, experience, and intellectual treasures from those in need. They are a center of goodness and blessing, and a gushing spring of the pure water of virtues that quenches the thirsty and makes the needy independent to the extent of their ability.

Indeed, their attributes are a collection of: firm conviction, strong faith, burning love for Allah, worship and obedience, effort and movement, and assistance in all dimensions to the servants of God.

Then, in the following verse, It addresses the great and significant reward of the true believers who possess the signs mentioned in the previous two verses. Using a beautiful expression that indicates the extraordinary importance of their reward, It says: "No soul knows what great rewards that are the delight of the eyes are hidden for them!" (فَلا تَعْلَمُ نَفْسٌ ما أُخْفِيَ لَهُمْ مِنْ قُرَّةِ أَعْيُنٍ). This extraordinarily great reward is "a recompense for what they used to do" (جَزاءً بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ).

The expression "No soul knows" and also the expression "qurrata a'yun" (that which is the delight/coolness of the eyes) indicate the boundless greatness of these bounties and rewards, especially considering that "nafs" (soul) appears as an indefinite noun in a negative context, which implies generality and includes all souls, even the archangels of God and the close friends (awliya) of the Lord.

The expression "qurrata a'yun" without being annexed to a specific "soul" suggests that these Divine blessings, which have been designated for the abode of the Hereafter as the reward for true believers, are such that they will bring light to the eyes of everyone.

"Qurrah" is from the root "qarr" (rhyming with harr), meaning coldness and coolness. Since it is well-known that tears of joy are always cool, while tears of grief and regret are hot and burning, the expression "qurrata a'yun" in the Arabic language means something that causes a person's eye to become cool—that is, it makes tears of joy flow from their eyes—and this is a subtle metaphor for ultimate happiness.

However, this specific idiom does not exist in Persian [in the same way]. Instead, we say: "It became the light of his eye." It is possible that this modern Persian expression is taken from the story of "Joseph" and "Jacob" in the Quran, where the Quran states that when the bearer of good news came to Jacob and cast Joseph's shirt over his face, his blind eyes suddenly became bright! (Surah Yusuf, Verse 96). This expression is also a metaphor for intense joy.

In a Hadith, we read that the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله says: "Allah says: I have prepared for My righteous servants what no eye has seen, no ear has heard, and has not crossed the heart of any human!" [^1] Here a question arises which the great commentator, the late Tabarsi, raised in "Majma' al-Bayan," which is: Why has this great reward been kept hidden?

He then mentions three answers to this question:

1- Important and highly valuable matters are such that their reality cannot be easily understood through words and speech; furthermore, sometimes their concealment and ambiguity are more exhilarating and more eloquent in terms of rhetoric.

2- Fundamentally, that which is the delight of the eyes is so vast in scope that human knowledge cannot encompass all its details.

3- Since this reward is set for the Night Prayer (Salat al-Layl), which is hidden, it is appropriate that the recompense for the deed be also great and hidden (note that in the previous verse, the phrase "تَتَجافى‌ جُنُوبُهُمْ عَنِ الْمَضاجِعِ" refers to the issue of the Night Prayer).

[^1]: This Hadith has been narrated by many commentators, including Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani, and Qurtubi in his Tafsir. The famous traditionists Bukhari and Muslim have also included it in their books.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"There is no good deed except that its reward is clearly stated in the Quran, except for the Night Prayer; for Allah, Exalted is His Name, has not clarified its reward due to its great importance. He said: فَلا تَعْلَمُ نَفْسٌ ما أُخْفِيَ لَهُمْ مِنْ قُرَّةِ أَعْيُنٍ: 'No soul knows what rewards that are the delight of the eyes are hidden for them.'" [^1]

But beyond all this, as we have previously pointed out, the world of the Resurrection is a world extraordinarily vaster than this world—even vaster than the life of this world compared to the life of a fetus in the mother's womb. Fundamentally, for us who are imprisoned within the four walls of this world, its dimensions are not perceivable, and it is not imaginable for anyone.

We only hear words about it and see a shadow from afar, but until we acquire the perception and vision of that world, understanding its importance is not possible for us, just as it is impossible for a child in the mother's womb—even assuming it had perfect intellect and intelligence—to understand the blessings of this world.

The same expression has appeared regarding the martyrs in the way of Allah: when the martyr falls upon the ground, the earth says, "Welcome to the pure soul that flies from a pure body; glad tidings be to you, for indeed you have what no eye has seen, no ear has heard, and has not crossed the heart of any human!" [^2]

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan" under the verses being discussed.

[^2]: "Majma' al-Bayan" under Verse 171 of Al-Imran, Vol. 2; Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 3, p. 172.

The next verse clarifies the comparison that was in the previous verses more explicitly, saying: "Is he who is a believer like him who is defiantly disobedient (fasiq)?! They are not equal" (أَ فَمَنْ كانَ مُؤْمِناً كَمَنْ كانَ فاسِقاً لا يَسْتَوُونَ).

The sentence is presented as a rhetorical question, a question whose answer gushes from the intellect and nature (fitrah) of every human: that these two are never equal. At the same time, for emphasis, it makes this inequality more distinct by mentioning the phrase "They are not equal."

In this verse, "fasiq" is placed in opposition to "believer," and this is evidence that "fisq" has a broad meaning that includes both disbelief (kufr) and other sins. This is because the word is originally derived from the phrase "fasaqat al-thamarah" (meaning the fruit came out of its skin, or in the case where the date pit separates from its flesh and falls out). Subsequently, it was applied to exiting from obedience to the command of Allah and the intellect. We know that whoever disbelieves or commits sins has exited from the command of the Lord and reason. This point is also noteworthy: as long as the fruit remains within its skin, it is healthy; as soon as it exits the skin, it becomes spoiled. Therefore, its becoming "fasiq" is synonymous with its becoming spoiled!

A group of great commentators have narrated under this verse that one day Walid bin Uqbah [^1] said to Ali عليه السلام: "I have a more expansive and eloquent tongue than you, and a sharper spearhead than you!" (implying that in his own imagination, he was more advanced in both oratory and warfare).

Ali عليه السلام replied to him:

"It is not as you say, O Fasiq (O transgressor)!" (referring to the fact that "you are the same person who, in the story of collecting zakat from the tribe of Bani al-Mustaliq, accused them of rising against Islam, and Allah in Surah al-Hujurat, Verse 6—'O you who have believed, if there comes to you a disobedient one (fasiq) with information, investigate...'—belied you and called you a fasiq.") [^1]

[^1]: "Uqbah" (rhyming with uqdah).

[^1]: This narration has been cited by the late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, Qurtubi in his Tafsir, and Fadil Barsawi in Ruh al-Bayan. It is noteworthy that in the book "Asad al-Ghabah fi Ma'rifat al-Sahabah," we read that among those knowledgeable in the interpretation of the Quran, there is no dispute that the verse "If a fasiq comes to you with news..." was revealed regarding Walid bin Uqbah in the story of the Bani al-Mustaliq tribe.

Some have added here that the verse "Is he who is a believer like him who is a fasiq" was revealed after this conversation. However, considering that the Surah under discussion (Surah as-Sajdah) was revealed in Mecca and the story of Walid and the Bani al-Mustaliq occurred in Medina, it seems that this is a case of applying the verse to a clear instance (misdaq).

But according to the view of some commentators who consider this verse and the two following it to be Medinan, no problem remains in this regard, and there is no obstacle to these three verses having been revealed after the aforementioned conversation.

In any case, there is no debate regarding the deep and rooted faith of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali bin Abi Talib عليه السلام, nor regarding the "fisq" of Walid, both of which have been alluded to in the verses of the Quran.

The next verse expresses this inequality in a broader manner, saying: "As for those who believed and did righteous deeds, for them will be the Gardens of Refuge as a permanent home" (أَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ فَلَهُمْ جَنَّاتُ الْمَأْوى‌) [^2]. Then It adds: "These Gardens of Refuge are a means of hospitality from Allah for them in return for the deeds they used to do" (نُزُلًا بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ).

[^2]: "Ma'wa" is from the root "awa" (rhyming with qawa), meaning the joining of one thing to another; subsequently, it was applied to a place, dwelling, and position.

The expression "nuzul," which is usually applied to something prepared for the reception of a guest, is a subtle allusion to the fact that the believers in Paradise are constantly hosted like guests, whereas the people of Hell—as will come in the next verse—are like prisoners who, whenever they desire to come out, are returned! And if we see that in Surah al-Kahf, Verse 102, it says: إِنَّا أَعْتَدْنا جَهَنَّمَ لِلْكافِرِينَ نُزُلًا: "Indeed, We have prepared Hell as a reception (nuzul) for the disbelievers," it is in fact of the same nature as "So give them tidings of a painful punishment"—a metaphor that instead of hospitality, He punishes them, and instead of good tidings, He threatens them.

Some believe that "nuzul" is the first thing with which a newly arrived guest is received (like tea or a drink in our time). Therefore, it is a subtle hint that the Gardens of Refuge, with all their blessings and bounties, are the first means of reception for these Divine guests, and following that are other favors and blessings that no one but Allah knows!

The expression "For them are Gardens" (فَلَهُمْ جَنَّاتُ) can also point to the fact that Allah does not give the gardens of Paradise to them as a loan, but rather grants them ownership forever, such that the possibility of these blessings ceasing never disturbs their peace of mind.

And in the next verse, It addresses their opposite point, saying: "But as for those who were defiantly disobedient (fasiqu) and exited from the obedience of their Lord, their permanent refuge is the Fire" (وَ أَمَّا الَّذِينَ فَسَقُوا فَمَأْواهُمُ النَّارُ).

They are imprisoned and confined in this terrifying place forever, such that: "Every time they wish to come out of it, they are returned into it" (كُلَّما أَرادُوا أَنْ يَخْرُجُوا مِنْها أُعِيدُوا فِيها).

And "it is said to them: Taste the punishment of the Fire which you used to deny" (وَ قِيلَ لَهُمْ ذُوقُوا عَذابَ النَّارِ الَّذِي كُنْتُمْ بِهِ تُكَذِّبُونَ).

Once again here, we see that Divine punishment is placed against "disbelief and denial," while His reward and recompense are placed against "action."

This is an indication that "faith" alone is not sufficient; rather, it must be a motive for action. However, "disbelief" alone is sufficient for punishment, even if no [bad] action accompanies it.

Key Points The Night-Watchers!

In the interpretation of the phrase "تَتَجافى‌ جُنُوبُهُمْ عَنِ الْمَضاجِعِ" (their sides forsake their beds at night), two interpretations have been mentioned in Islamic narrations:

One interpretation refers to the "Isha" prayer, indicating that true believers do not go to bed after the Maghrib prayer and before the Isha prayer, lest sleep overtake them and their Isha prayer be missed. (Because at that time, it was customary to rest at the beginning of the night and to separate the Maghrib and Isha prayers—according to the recommended instruction of separating the five daily prayers—performing each in its virtuous time). Whenever they slept after Maghrib and before the time of Isha, it was possible they would not wake up for the Isha prayer.

This interpretation was narrated by Ibn Abbas from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله as cited in "Ad-Durr al-Manthur," and it is also narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام in the "Amali" of the Shaykh [^1]. However, in most narrations and the words of commentators, it is interpreted as rising from the bed to perform the Night Prayer (Salat al-Layl).

In a narration from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read that he said to one of his companions:

"Shall I not inform you of the root of Islam, its branch, and its highest peak?"

The narrator said: "Please do, may I be sacrificed for you."

He said: "As for its root, it is prayer (Salat); its branch is Zakat; and its highest peak is Jihad!"

Then he added: "If you wish, I shall inform you of all the gates of goodness."

The narrator says: "Please do, may I be sacrificed for you."

The Imam said: "Fasting is a shield, and charity removes sin, and a man's standing in the middle of the night in remembrance of Allah." Then he recited: "تَتَجافى‌ جُنُوبُهُمْ عَنِ الْمَضاجِعِ" [^2].

In the commentary "Majma' al-Bayan," it is narrated from Mu'adh bin Jabal: "I was in the service of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله during the Battle of Tabuk. The heat had severely distressed everyone, and each person sought refuge in a corner. Suddenly, I saw that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was closer to me than anyone else. I went to him and said: 'O Messenger of Allah! Teach me an action that will enter me into Paradise and distance me from the Fire of Hell.'"

He said: "You have asked a great question, but its answer is not difficult for one for whom Allah has made it easy." Then he added: "Worship Allah and do not associate anything with Him, establish the obligatory prayers, pay the prescribed Zakat—the right of the poor—and fast the month of Ramadan."

Then he said: "And if you wish, I will inform you of the gates of goodness." I said: "O Prophet, please do." He said: "Fasting is a shield from the Fire, and charity in the way of Allah is an expiation for sins, and a man's standing in the middle of the night seeking the pleasure of Allah." Then he recited this verse: "تَتَجافى‌ جُنُوبُهُمْ عَنِ الْمَضاجِعِ" [^1].

[^1]: "Ad-Durr al-Manthur" and "Amali of the Shaykh" according to "Tafsir al-Mizan," Vol. 16, p. 283.

[^2]: "Usul al-Kafi," Vol. 2, Chapter "Da'aim al-Islam," Hadith 15, p. 20, and the previous source.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan under the verses being discussed and Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 229.

Although there is no obstacle to the verse having a broad meaning—including staying awake at the beginning of the night for the Isha prayer and rising at dawn for the Night Prayer—a careful look at the concept of "tatajafa" reflects the second meaning more strongly in the mind. This is because the literal sense of the sentence is that the sides were previously settled in the bed and then moved away from it, which is suitable for rising in the latter part of the night to perform prayer. Therefore, the first set of narrations is of the nature of expanding the concept and removing specific restrictions.

Although those few narrations above seem sufficient regarding the importance of this blessed prayer, it is noteworthy that in Islamic narrations, so much importance has been given to this act of worship that few other acts of worship have been spoken of in such a manner.

The friends of the Truth and the wayfarers of the path of virtue always gave great importance to this sincere worship, which brings light to the heart and illumination and purity to the soul.

It is possible that some may not always have the success (tawfiq) to benefit from this blessed worship, but what is the harm in benefiting from it on some nights when this success is attained? At that time when silence has enveloped everywhere, daily occupations are all suspended, children are asleep, and the environment is ready for the presence of the heart and intimate prayer with God, they should rise, go to the door of God's house, and illuminate their hearts with the light of the Beloved's love [^1].

[^1]: Regarding the importance of the Night Prayer and its method, we have had another discussion in Vol. 12, p. 227 onwards (under Verse 79 of Surah al-Isra).

Ayahs

وَلَنُذِيقَنَّهُم مِّنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ ٱلْأَدْنَىٰ دُونَ ٱلْعَذَابِ ٱلْأَكْبَرِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (٢١)
We shall surely make them taste the nearer punishment prior to the greater punishment, so that they may come back. (21)
وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّن ذُكِّرَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِۦ ثُمَّ أَعْرَضَ عَنْهَآ ۚ إِنَّا مِنَ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ مُنتَقِمُونَ (٢٢)
Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who is reminded of his Lord’s signs, whereat he disregards them? Indeed, We shall take vengeance upon the guilty. (22)

Commentary

Educational Punishments

Following the discussion regarding sinners and their painful punishment in the preceding verses, the verses under discussion point to one of the subtle favors of Allah toward them, which is the mild and awakening worldly punishments. This is to make it clear that Allah never desires for a servant to be trapped in eternal torment; for this reason, He utilizes all awakening means to save them.

He sends Divine Prophets, reveals Heavenly Books, bestows blessings, and afflicts them with calamities. If none of these prove beneficial, such individuals will have no destiny other than the fire of Hell.

He says: "And We will surely make them taste of the nearer punishment [of this world] before the greater punishment [of the Hereafter], that perhaps they may return [to the right path]" (وَ لَنُذِيقَنَّهُمْ مِنَ الْعَذابِ الْأَدْنى‌ دُونَ الْعَذابِ الْأَكْبَرِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ‌).

Certainly, "the nearer punishment" (al-adhab al-adna) has a broad meaning that encompasses most of the possibilities that commentators have written separately in its interpretation.

Among these is that it refers to misfortunes, pain, and suffering.

Or the severe seven-year famine and drought that the polytheists in Mecca were afflicted with, to the point that they were forced to feed on carcasses! Or the severe blow dealt to them in the Battle of Badr.

And things of this nature.

However, the possibility suggested by some that it refers to the "punishment of the grave" or "punishment during the Return (Raj'ah)" does not seem correct, as it is inconsistent with the phrase "that perhaps they may return" (لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ‌).

Of course, one must note that in this world, there are also punishments during which the doors of repentance are closed; these are the "punishments of extermination" (adhab al-istisal). These are punishments sent to destroy rebellious nations when no means of awakening remains effective upon them, and naturally, this type of punishment is also outside the scope of this verse.

As for "the greater punishment" (al-adhab al-akbar), it refers to the punishment of the Day of Resurrection, which is greater and more painful than any other punishment.

Regarding why "adna" (nearer) is placed in contrast to "akbar" (greater)—whereas "adna" should typically be contrasted with "ab'ad" (farther), or "asghar" (smaller) with "akbar"—there is a point that some commentators have noted:

The punishment of this world possesses two attributes: "being small" and "being near." When issuing a threat, it is not appropriate to emphasize its smallness; rather, its nearness should be emphasized.

The punishment of the Hereafter also possesses two attributes: "being far" and "being great." In its case, it is appropriate to emphasize its "greatness" rather than its "farness" (reflect upon this).

The use of the expression "la'alla" (perhaps) in the phrase "that perhaps they may return" (لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ‌)—as we have mentioned before—is because tasting the warning punishments is not the "sufficient cause" (al-illat al-tammah) for awakening, but rather a part of the cause. it requires a prepared ground, and without this condition, it does not reach the result; the word "perhaps" points to this reality.

Furthermore, from this verse, one of the important philosophies of misfortunes, calamities, and sufferings—which is among the most question-provoking issues in the discussion of Monotheism, the knowledge of God, and Divine Justice—becomes clear.

This reality is pointed out not only here but in other verses of the Quran as well. For instance, in verse 94 of Surah Al-A'raf, we read: وَ ما أَرْسَلْنا فِي قَرْيَةٍ مِنْ نَبِيٍّ إِلَّا أَخَذْنا أَهْلَها بِالْبَأْساءِ وَ الضَّرَّاءِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَضَّرَّعُونَ‌: "And We sent no prophet to a city except that We seized its people with adversity and hardship that they might humble themselves [to Allah]."

And since whenever none of the awakening means, even Divine punishment, prove beneficial, there remains no way but the vengeance of the Lord upon this group who are the most unjust of individuals, He says in the following verse:

"And who is more unjust than he who is reminded of the verses of his Lord and then turns away from them?" (وَ مَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنْ ذُكِّرَ بِآياتِ رَبِّهِ ثُمَّ أَعْرَضَ عَنْها).

"Indeed, We will take retribution from the faithless criminals" (إِنَّا مِنَ الْمُجْرِمِينَ مُنْتَقِمُونَ‌).

These are, in truth, those upon whom neither Divine favors and blessings have had an effect, nor His warning punishments and calamities. Therefore, there is no one more unjust than them, and if retribution is not taken from them, from whom should it be taken?! It is clear that, considering the previous verses, the "criminals" (mujrimin) here refers to those who deny the Origin or the Resurrection and the faithless sinners.

In the verses of the Quran, various groups are repeatedly introduced as "azlam" (the most unjust), and although the expressions vary, they all actually return to the root of disbelief, polytheism, and lack of faith. Therefore, the concept of "the most unjust," which is a superlative adjective, is not undermined.

The use of the word "thumma" (then) in the above verse, which usually denotes a time interval, may indicate that such individuals are given sufficient opportunity for study; their initial opposition never triggers Divine retribution. However, after the necessary opportunity has expired, they will deserve the vengeance of Allah.

Additionally, it should be noted that the term "intiqam" (retribution/vengeance) in the Arabic language means "to punish," even though the concept of "healing the heart" (soothing an inner sting) has entered the common usage of this word; however, it does not exist in its original linguistic meaning.

Therefore, this expression is used repeatedly in the Holy Quran regarding Allah the Almighty, while He is far above and beyond such human concepts; He acts only out of Wisdom.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ فَلَا تَكُن فِى مِرْيَةٍ مِّن لِّقَآئِهِۦ ۖ وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُ هُدًى لِّبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ (٢٣)
Certainly We gave Moses the Book, [declaring], ‘Do not be in doubt about the encounter with Him,’ and We made it a [source of] guidance for the Children of Israel. (23)
وَجَعَلْنَا مِنْهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنَا لَمَّا صَبَرُوا۟ ۖ وَكَانُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يُوقِنُونَ (٢٤)
When they had been patient and had conviction in Our signs, We appointed amongst them imams to guide [the people] by Our command. (24)
إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فِيمَا كَانُوا۟ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ (٢٥)
Indeed your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ. (25)

Commentary

Patience, Endurance, and Faith—The Capital of Imamat

The verses under discussion provide a brief and passing reference to the story of Moses (عليه السلام) and the Children of Israel to console and comfort the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the early believers. They serve as a call to patience, endurance, and steadfastness in the face of the denials, rejections, and obstructions of the polytheists mentioned in the preceding verses. Furthermore, they offer glad tidings to the believers that they will ultimately prevail over this stubborn group of disbelievers, just as the Children of Israel triumphed over their enemies and became leaders on the earth.

Since Moses (عليه السلام) is a great prophet in whom both Jews and Christians believe, this reference can also serve as an incentive for the People of the Book to move toward the Quran and Islam.

First, He says: "We certainly gave Moses the Scripture" (وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا مُوسَى الْكِتَابَ).

"Therefore, harbor no doubt that Moses received the Divine verses" (فَلَا تَكُنْ فِي مِرْيَةٍ مِنْ لِقَائِهِ).

"And We made the heavenly book of Moses, the Torah, a means of guidance for the Children of Israel" (وَجَعَلْنَاهُ هُدًى لِبَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ).

There is much discussion among commentators regarding what the pronoun in "مِنْ لِقَائِهِ" (meeting/receiving it) refers to, with seven or more possibilities suggested.

However, the most plausible among them is that it refers to "the Book" (the heavenly book of Moses, i.e., the Torah), functioning as the object, while the subject is Moses.

Thus, the meaning of the entire sentence is: "Do not doubt that Moses attained the meeting of the heavenly Book and received that which was bestowed upon him from the presence of the Lord."

A clear piece of evidence for this interpretation is that three sentences appear in the above verse: the first and the last certainly speak of the Torah. Therefore, it is appropriate that the middle sentence follows the same theme, rather than speaking of the Resurrection or the Holy Quran, which would make it a parenthetical statement. We know that parenthetical statements are contrary to the apparent meaning and should not be resorted to unless necessary.

The only remaining question in this interpretation is the use of the word "liqa" (meeting) regarding a heavenly book, as this word in the Quran is mostly used in conjunction with "Allah," "Lord," or "the Hereafter," referring to the Resurrection.

For this reason, some have preferred the possibility that the verse first speaks of the revelation of the Torah to Moses (عليه السلام), then commands the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) not to doubt "the meeting with Allah and the issue of the Resurrection," and then returns again to the subject of the Torah.

However, it must be admitted that in this case, the harmony between the sentences of this verse would be completely disrupted, and its cohesion shattered.

Yet, it should be noted that although the expression "liqa" is not used in the Quran regarding the reception of heavenly books, the terms "ilqa" (bestowing/casting) and "talaqqi" (receiving) are frequently used in this sense. As we read in Surah Al-Qamar, verse 25: أَأُلْقِيَ الذِّكْرُ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ بَيْنِنَا ("Has the Reminder been bestowed upon him alone among us?"). And in the story of Solomon and the Queen of Sheba, when Solomon's letter reached her, she said: إِنِّي أُلْقِيَ إِلَيَّ كِتَابٌ كَرِيمٌ ("Indeed, a noble letter has been delivered to me") (Naml: 29).

And in this very Surah (An-Naml), verse 6, we read regarding the Holy Quran: وَإِنَّكَ لَتُلَقَّى الْقُرْآنَ مِنْ لَدُنْ حَكِيمٍ عَلِيمٍ ("And indeed, you receive the Quran from the presence of One Wise and Knowing").

Therefore, the verbs "ilqa" and "talaqqi" are repeatedly used in this context.

Even the verb "liqa" itself is used regarding the record of human deeds in the Holy Quran; in Surah Al-Isra, verse 13, we read: وَنُخْرِجُ لَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ كِتَابًا يَلْقَاهُ مَنْشُورًا ("And We will bring forth for him on the Day of Resurrection a record which he will find wide open"). From the sum of what we have said, the preference of this interpretation over other possibilities given for the verse becomes clear [1]. However, it is necessary to note that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) had no doubt or hesitation in such matters; rather, such expressions are usually for emphasizing the point and serving as a model for others.

[1]: A group of commentators considered the reference of the pronoun in "liqa'ihi" to be Moses (عليه السلام); thus, the meaning would be: "Do not doubt, O Muhammad, that you will meet Moses," referring to meeting him on the Night of Ascension (Mi'raj) or on the Day of Resurrection. This meaning seems inconsistent with the context of the sentence. Others have said: The reference of the pronoun is "the Book," meaning the Quran—i.e., "You, O Prophet, do not harbor doubt that this Quran is Divine revelation." Although this meaning aligns with the opening verses of this Surah, it does not fit well with the other sentences in this verse. Furthermore, "the Book" in the verse under discussion refers to the Torah, and returning the pronoun to the Quran lacks harmony; justifying this by saying it refers to "heavenly books in general" does not diminish its departure from the apparent meaning. Other commentators have said: The pronoun in "liqa'ihi" refers to Allah, and this sentence indicates: "Do not doubt the matter of the Resurrection." This meaning, while not inconsistent with previous verses, has almost no harmony with the content of the verse itself. From this, it becomes clear that the interpretation in some commentaries suggesting the verse refers to the meeting of the two paths of Moses and the Prophet of Islam is a matter of personal taste (dhawqi) that does not align with the actual meaning of the words. Therefore, the clearest interpretation is the one we provided above.

In the next verse, He points to the honors bestowed upon the Children of Israel in the shadow of steadfastness and faith, so it may be a lesson for others. He says: "And We appointed from among them leaders guiding by Our command when they endured patiently and were certain of Our signs" (وَجَعَلْنَا مِنْهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنَا لَمَّا صَبَرُوا وَكَانُوا بِآيَاتِنَا يُوقِنُونَ).

Here, the secret of victory and the condition for leadership and Imamat are identified as two things: first, faith and certainty in Divine signs, and second, patience, steadfastness, and endurance.

This matter is not exclusive to the Children of Israel; it is a lesson for all nations and for Muslims of yesterday, today, and tomorrow—to strengthen the foundations of their certainty and not to fear the difficulties in the path of bringing the line of Monotheism to fruition. They must adopt patience and resistance so that they may become leaders of the people and guides of nations in the history of the world.

The use of "يَهْدُونَ" (they guide) in the present continuous tense, as well as the phrase "يُوقِنُونَ" (they have certainty) also in the present continuous, indicates the continuity of these two attributes throughout their lives. This is because the matter of leadership is never free from difficulties for a moment, and at every step, the leader and guide of the people faces a new challenge that must be combated with the power of certainty and continuous endurance to maintain the line of guidance by Divine command.

It is noteworthy that He restricts the matter of guidance to "Divine command," saying: يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنَا. The crucial point in guidance is that it must stem from Divine command, not from the command of the people, personal whims and desires, or the imitation of others.

Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), in a profound Hadith utilizing the themes of the Holy Quran, divides leaders into two types:

"Leaders who undertake guidance by the command of God, not by the command of the people; they prioritize the command of God over their own command and His judgment over their own judgment. And leaders who invite to the Fire; they prioritize their own command over the command of Truth and their own judgment before the Divine judgment, acting according to their whims and contrary to the Book of Allah" [1].

Regarding whether "command" (amr) here refers to the legislative command (Divine instructions in Sharia) or the creative command (the influence of Divine will in the realm of creation), the apparent meaning of the verse is the former, and the expressions in narrations and commentaries also evoke this meaning.

However, some great commentators have considered it to mean the "creative command" (amr takwini).

To explain: Guidance in verses and narrations appears in two senses: "showing the way" (ira'at al-tariq) and "delivering to the goal" (isal ila al-matlub).

The guidance of Divine leaders also occurs through both ways. Sometimes they suffice with commanding and forbidding, but sometimes, through an inner influence on worthy and prepared hearts, they lead them to educational goals and spiritual stations.

The word "amr" in some verses of the Quran is used to mean "creative command," such as: إِنَّمَا أَمْرُهُ إِذَا أَرَادَ شَيْئًا أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ ("His command is only, when He intends a thing, that He says to it, 'Be,' and it is") (Surah Ya-Sin, verse 82).

The phrase "يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنَا" in the verse under discussion also points to this meaning—that they were leaders who, by the power of the Lord, influenced prepared souls and steered them toward high educational and human goals [2]. This meaning is inherently significant and is one of the functions of Imamat and branches of guidance. However, restricting the phrase "يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنَا" solely to this meaning is not consistent with the apparent text of the verse. Yet, there is no obstacle to taking "amr" in this sentence in its broad sense, encompassing both the "creative" and "legislative" commands, so that both meanings of guidance are combined in the verse. This meaning is also harmonious with some Hadiths received regarding the interpretation of the verse.

In any case, an Imam and leader reaching this station is only possible in the light of certainty and steadfastness.

The remaining question here is whether these leaders among the Children of Israel refer to the prophets who existed among them, or if the scholars and sages who guided people to goodness by Divine command are also included.

The verse is silent on this; it only says We appointed a group of them as guiding leaders. However, considering the word "جَعَلْنَا" (We appointed/made), it seems more likely to refer to the prophets who were appointed to this station by God.

[1]: "Indeed, the leaders in the Book of Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, are of two types: Allah, Blessed and Exalted, said: 'And We made them leaders guiding by Our command,' not by the command of the people; they put the command of Allah before their own command and the judgment of Allah before their own judgment. And He said: 'And We made them leaders who invite to the Fire'; they put their own command before the command of Allah and their own judgment before the judgment of Allah, and they follow their whims contrary to what is in the Book of Allah, the Almighty and Majestic." (Kafi, Vol. 1, p. 168, Chapter: The Leaders in the Book of Allah are of two types).

[2]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 1, p. 275.

Since the Children of Israel—like other nations—resorted to disagreement after these true leaders and guides, followed various paths, and became the source of division among the people, the final verse under discussion says in a threatening tone: "Indeed, your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that over which they used to differ," and will give everyone the recompense for their deeds (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ فِيمَا كَانُوا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ).

Yes, the source of disagreement is always the mixing of "Truth" with "whims and desires." Since the Resurrection is a day when whims and desires lose their color and vanish, and Truth manifests as it truly is, it is there that Allah ends the disagreements by His command. This is another philosophy of the Resurrection (reflect on this).

Key Point: The Patience and Steadfastness of Divine Leaders

We mentioned that in the verses under discussion, two conditions are stated for leaders and Imams:

First, patience and endurance, and second, faith and certainty in Divine signs.

This patience and endurance have many branches: sometimes it is in the face of calamities that befall the person himself.

Sometimes it is against the harassment directed at his friends and followers.

Sometimes it is against the insults and foul language directed at his sanctities.

Sometimes it comes from the narrow-minded.

Sometimes from the ill-intentioned.

Sometimes from the ignorant.

And sometimes from the knowledgeable who harbor ill will! In short, an aware leader must endure and remain steadfast in the face of all these problems and more. He must never flee the field of events, never show restlessness or grief, never lose self-control, never become despondent, and never become anxious or regretful, until he attains his great goal.

In this regard, a comprehensive and interesting Hadith has been narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) which is necessary to mention.

He said to one of his friends: "Whoever is patient, is patient for a short time (followed by victory), and whoever is restless, is restless for a short time (ending in defeat)." Then he said: "Upon you be patience and endurance in all matters, for Allah the Great sent Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) and commanded him to have patience and leniency, saying: 'Endure patiently what they say and avoid them in a becoming manner'—but not an avoidance that prevents inviting to the Truth."

He also said: "Counter evil with good, for then he between whom and you was enmity will become as though he was a devoted friend. And none shall reach this station except those who are patient and those who possess a great portion of faith."

He then added: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) adopted patience until they fired various arrows of accusation at him (calling him mad, a sorcerer, a poet, and denying his prophethood). The Prophet's chest became constricted by their words. Allah revealed this word to him: 'We know that your chest is constricted by what they say, but celebrate the praise of your Lord and be among those who prostrate' (for these acts of worship will grant you tranquility)."

Once again they denied him and accused him; he became sorrowful. Allah revealed this word to him: "We know that their words grieve you, but know that their goal is not to deny you; rather, these wrongdoers deny the signs of Allah. Messengers before you were denied but they were patient; they were harmed and they endured until Our help came." The Prophet continued to be patient until they exceeded the limits, mentioned the name of Allah wickedly, and denied Him.

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "O Allah! I have been patient regarding myself, my family, and my honor, but I cannot be patient against the vilification of Your Holy Station." Again, Allah commanded him to be patient, saying: "Be patient over what they say."

He then adds: Following that, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was patient in all states and against all difficulties, and this became the reason for God to give him the glad tidings that from his progeny there would be Imams and leaders, and He recommended patience to these Imams as well...

It was here that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: الصبر من الايمان كالرأس من الجسد "Patience is to faith as the head is to the body." Ultimately, this steadfastness and patience led to his victory over the polytheists, and the command to take retribution against those oppressors who were beyond guidance was issued. Their life's scroll was folded by the hands of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and his companions. This was their reward in this world, in addition to the rewards stored for him in the Hereafter.

Then Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) added: فمن صبر و احتسب لم يخرج من الدنيا حتى يقر اللَّه له عينا فى اعدائه مع ما يدخر له فى الآخرة "Whoever is patient and seeks reward from Allah will not leave this world until Allah delights his eyes with the defeat of his enemies, in addition to the rewards He will store for his Hereafter" [1].

[1]: "Usul al-Kafi," Vol. 2, p. 72, Chapter on Patience (with slight abbreviation).

Ayahs

أَوَلَمْ يَهْدِ لَهُمْ كَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّنَ ٱلْقُرُونِ يَمْشُونَ فِى مَسَـٰكِنِهِمْ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ ۖ أَفَلَا يَسْمَعُونَ (٢٦)
Does it not dawn upon them how many generations We have destroyed before them, amid [the ruins of] whose dwellings they walk? There are indeed signs in that. Will they not then listen? (26)
أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّا نَسُوقُ ٱلْمَآءَ إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ ٱلْجُرُزِ فَنُخْرِجُ بِهِۦ زَرْعًا تَأْكُلُ مِنْهُ أَنْعَـٰمُهُمْ وَأَنفُسُهُمْ ۖ أَفَلَا يُبْصِرُونَ (٢٧)
Do they not see that We carry water to the parched earth and with it We bring forth crops, from which they eat, themselves and their cattle? Will they not then see? (27)
وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَـٰذَا ٱلْفَتْحُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٢٨)
They say, ‘When will this judgement be, if you are truthful?’ (28)
قُلْ يَوْمَ ٱلْفَتْحِ لَا يَنفَعُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِيمَـٰنُهُمْ وَلَا هُمْ يُنظَرُونَ (٢٩)
Say, ‘On the day of judgement their [newly found] faith will not avail the faithless, nor will they be granted any respite.’ (29)
فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ وَٱنتَظِرْ إِنَّهُم مُّنتَظِرُونَ (٣٠)
So turn away from them, and wait. They too are waiting. (30)

Commentary

The Day of Our Victory

The preceding verses were intertwined with threats against the faithless criminals, and the first verse under discussion serves as an explanation and completion of this threat. It says: "Is it not a guidance for them [to see] how many generations We destroyed before them, and We brought them to the punishment for their deeds?" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَهْدِ لَهُمْ كَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ مِنَ الْقُرُونِ) [^1] "They walk in their [ruined] dwellings" and see the traces of those cursed nations with their own eyes (يَمْشُونَ فِي مَسَاكِنِهِمْ) [^2]. The disaster-stricken lands of "Ad" and "Thamud" and the ruined cities of the "People of Lot" lie on their path to Sham (the Levant). Whenever they pass through these lands—which were once centers for powerful yet misguided and corrupt nations, who ignored every warning from their prophets until divine punishment finally folded the scroll of their lives—it is as if every pebble in the desert and every ruined palace possesses a hundred tongues, crying out and recounting the consequences of disbelief and corruption. Yet, it seems they have completely lost their hearing.

Therefore, at the end of the verse, it adds: "Indeed, in that are signs of Allah's power and lessons to be learned; do they not then hear?" (إِنَّ فِي ذَلِكَ لَآيَاتٍ أَفَلَا يَسْمَعُونَ).

In the next verse, it points to one of the most important divine blessings, which is the source of prosperity for all lands and the means of life for all living beings, so it may become clear that just as Allah has the power to destroy the lands of the wicked, He is capable of reviving desolate and dead lands and granting all kinds of bounties to His servants.

It says: "Have they not seen that We drive the water to the parched land, devoid of herbage, and thereby bring forth crops from which their livestock eat and they themselves? Do they not then see?" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّا نَسُوقُ الْمَاءَ إِلَى الْأَرْضِ الْجُرُزِ فَنُخْرِجُ بِهِ زَرْعًا تَأْكُلُ مِنْهُ أَنْعَامُهُمْ وَأَنْفُسُهُمْ أَفَلَا يُبْصِرُونَ).

"Juruz" (rhyming with shatur) refers to land where vegetation has been uprooted, or in other words, land where no plants grow at all. Originally, it comes from the root "jarz" (rhyming with maradh), meaning to cut or sever. It is as if every kind of plant has been cut off from such land, or the land itself has severed those plants.

Interestingly, the expression used here is "We drive the water" (نَسُوقُ الْمَاءَ). This points to the fact that the nature of water, due to its weight, requires it to remain on the ground or in hollows, and due to its fluidity, it should sink into the depths of the earth. However, when Our command arrives, it loses its [heavy] nature and transforms into light vapor, which moves in every direction with the blowing of the breeze.

Yes, these very clouds above the sky are vast oceans of fresh water that, by Allah's command and with the help of the winds, are sent toward parched lands. Truly, if it did not rain, many lands would not see a single drop of water; even if, hypothetically, there were full rivers, they would not reach [the high lands]. But now we see that by the grace of this divine mercy, abundant forests, trees, and plants have grown atop many mountains, difficult slopes, and high hills. This extraordinary power of irrigation belongs only to rain and cannot be achieved by anything else.

"Zar'" (crop/vegetation) here has a broad meaning that includes every kind of plant and tree, although it is sometimes used in contrast to trees.

Placing "livestock" before "humans" in this verse may be because the entire nutrition of livestock comes from plants, whereas humans feed both on plants and the meat of livestock. Or, it could be because plants are usable by livestock as soon as they sprout, while human use of plants is often deferred to a later time when they bear grain and fruit.

It is noteworthy that at the end of the verse under discussion, the phrase "Do they not then see?" (أَفَلَا يُبْصِرُونَ) is used, whereas at the end of the previous verse, which spoke of the ruined palaces and houses of past nations, the phrase "Do they not then hear?" (أَفَلَا يَسْمَعُونَ) was used. This difference is because the sight of dead lands coming to life due to rainfall is something everyone sees with their eyes, whereas matters concerning past nations are mostly heard as reports.

In a summary of these two verses, it is understood that Allah says to this rebellious group: Open your eyes and ears, hear and see the truths, and reflect on how one day We commanded the winds to crush and destroy the palaces of the people of Ad, and another day We command these same winds to carry rain-laden clouds to dead and barren lands to make them prosperous and green. Will you still not submit before such power?!

Since the previous verses threatened the criminals with vengeance and gave the believers glad tidings of leadership and victory, here the disbelievers, out of pride and arrogance, pose this question: When will these promises and threats be fulfilled? As the Quran says: "And they say: 'When will this victory be, if you are truthful?'" (وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَى هَذَا الْفَتْحُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صَادِقِينَ).

The Quran immediately answers them and commands the Prophet: "Say: On the Day of Victory, the faith of those who disbelieved will not profit them, nor will they be given respite!" (قُلْ يَوْمَ الْفَتْحِ لَا يَنْفَعُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِيمَانُهُمْ وَلَا هُمْ يُنْظَرُونَ). This means that if your intention is to see the truth of the divine promises you heard from the Prophet and then believe, by that day it will be too late, and faith will be of no benefit to you.

From what we have said, it becomes clear that "The Day of Victory" (يَوْمَ الْفَتْحِ) refers to the day of the descent of "the uprooting punishment" (adhab al-istisal); that is, a punishment that roots out the disbelievers and leaves no room for them to believe. In other words, this uprooting punishment is a type of worldly punishment—not the punishment of the Hereafter, nor the ordinary worldly punishments—that ends the lives of sinful nations after the completion of the proof (itmam al-hujjah).

Several points testify to this

A: If the intention were ordinary worldly punishments or victories like the victory of the Muslims in the Battle of Badr or the day of the Conquest of Mecca—as some commentators have suggested—the phrase "the faith of those who disbelieved will not profit them" would not be correct. This is because on both the day of Badr and the day of the Conquest of Mecca, accepting faith was beneficial and the doors of repentance were open.

B: If "The Day of Victory" referred to the Day of Resurrection, as some commentators have accepted, it would not be consistent with the phrase "nor will they be given respite" (وَلَا هُمْ يُنْظَرُونَ), because giving or not giving respite pertains to the life of this world. Furthermore, in no instance in the Quran is "The Day of Victory" used to mean the Resurrection.

C: The term "Victory" (fath) is repeatedly used in the Holy Quran in reference to "the uprooting punishment," such as in verse 118 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara, where Noah says: "فَافْتَحْ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَهُمْ فَتْحًا وَنَجِّنِي وَمَنْ مَعِيَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ" ("O my Lord! Judge between me and them and root out this oppressive nation, and save me and all the believers who are with me"), which refers to the punishment of the Flood. A similar meaning appears in verse 77 of Surah Al-Mu'minun.

However, if the intention is the uprooting punishment in this world, it aligns with what we have stated above and is consistent with all the aforementioned evidence. In reality, it is a threat to the disbelievers and oppressors: do not keep requesting the fulfillment of the promise of victory for the believers and the uprooting punishment for the disbelievers, for if your request is granted, no respite will be given to you, nor will your faith be accepted if you find the chance to believe.

This meaning is particularly harmonious with the preceding verses regarding the destruction of rebellious nations who lived in previous centuries and were seized by divine punishment and annihilated. For the disbelievers of Mecca, upon hearing what was mentioned in the two previous verses, would naturally—out of stubbornness—request such a thing for themselves. But the Quran warns them never to request such a thing, for if the punishment descends, nothing will remain of you.

Finally, in the last verse of this Surah (Surah As-Sajdah), the discourse concludes with an eloquent and meaningful threat, saying: "O Prophet! Now that it is so, turn away from them and wait; indeed, they too are waiting" (فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ وَانْتَظِرْ إِنَّهُمْ مُنْتَظِرُونَ). Now that neither glad tidings nor warnings affect them, and they are neither people of logic and reasoning—to recognize Him by observing divine signs in the expanse of creation and worship none but Him—nor do they possess an awakened conscience to listen to the melody of monotheism from within their souls, turn away from them. You wait for Allah's mercy, and they wait for His punishment, for they are worthy only of punishment!

O Lord! Make us among those who submit and believe upon seeing the first signs of truth. O Allah! Remove the spirit of pride, arrogance, and stubbornness from all of us. O God! Grant the complete victory of the army of Islam over the army of disbelief, arrogance, and colonialism as soon as possible.

End of Surah As-Sajdah 3rd of Muharram al-Haram 1404 - October 10, 1983

[^1]: The subject of "lam yahdi" is the concept derived from the phrase "how many We destroyed before them," and the implied meaning is: "Has the multitude of those We destroyed before them not guided them?" [^2]: Most commentators have provided the interpretation of the verse as we mentioned above, but some have suggested the possibility that the phrase "they walk" describes the state of those who were destroyed; meaning, while they were completely unaware of divine punishment and were walking in their dwellings, the divine punishment suddenly arrived and destroyed them. However, this possibility seems unlikely.
